From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

1980 film directed by Martin Scorsese

This article is about the 1980 film. For other uses, see [Raging Bull (disambiguation)](/wiki/Raging_Bull_(disambiguation) "Raging Bull (disambiguation)").

***Raging Bull*** is a 1980 American [biographical](/wiki/Biographical_film "Biographical film") [sports](/wiki/Sports_film "Sports film") [drama film](/wiki/Drama_film "Drama film") directed by [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese") from a screenplay by [Paul Schrader](/wiki/Paul_Schrader "Paul Schrader") and [Mardik Martin](/wiki/Mardik_Martin "Mardik Martin"), which adapts [Jake LaMotta](/wiki/Jake_LaMotta "Jake LaMotta")'s 1970 memoir *[Raging Bull: My Story](/wiki/Raging_Bull:_My_Story "Raging Bull: My Story")*. [Robert De Niro](/wiki/Robert_De_Niro "Robert De Niro") stars as LaMotta, a former [middleweight](/wiki/Middleweight "Middleweight") boxing champion whose turbulent personal life is beset by rage and jealousy. Scorsese dedicated the film to Armenian-American NYU film professor [Haig P. Manoogian](/wiki/Haig_P._Manoogian "Haig P. Manoogian").[[4]](#cite_note-4) The supporting cast includes [Joe Pesci](/wiki/Joe_Pesci "Joe Pesci"), [Cathy Moriarty](/wiki/Cathy_Moriarty "Cathy Moriarty"), [Theresa Saldana](/wiki/Theresa_Saldana "Theresa Saldana"), [Frank Vincent](/wiki/Frank_Vincent "Frank Vincent") and [Nicholas Colasanto](/wiki/Nicholas_Colasanto "Nicholas Colasanto") (in his final film role).

Scorsese was initially reluctant to develop the project, although he eventually came to relate with LaMotta's story. Schrader rewrote Martin's first screenplay, and Scorsese and De Niro together made uncredited contributions thereafter. Pesci was a relatively unknown actor prior to the film, as was Moriarty, whom Pesci recommended. During [principal photography](/wiki/Principal_photography "Principal photography"), each of the boxing scenes was choreographed for a specific visual style, and De Niro gained approximately 60 pounds (27 kg) to portray LaMotta in his later years. Scorsese was exacting in the process of [editing and mixing](/wiki/Post-production "Post-production") the film, expecting it to be his last major feature.

*Raging Bull* premiered in New York City on November 14, 1980, and was released in theaters on December 19. The film had a lukewarm box office of $23.4 million against its $18 million budget, and received mixed reviews from critics. While De Niro's performance and the editing were widely acclaimed, its violent content received criticism. *Raging Bull* received a leading eight nominations at the [53rd Academy Awards](/wiki/53rd_Academy_Awards "53rd Academy Awards"), including [Best Picture](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Picture "Academy Award for Best Picture"), and won two: [Best Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actor "Academy Award for Best Actor") for De Niro and [Best Editing](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Film_Editing "Academy Award for Best Film Editing") for [Thelma Schoonmaker](/wiki/Thelma_Schoonmaker "Thelma Schoonmaker").

In the decades since its release, *Raging Bull* has come to be considered one of the [greatest films ever made](/wiki/Greatest_films_ever_made "Greatest films ever made"). In 1990, it became the first film to be selected in its first year of eligibility for preservation in the US [National Film Registry](/wiki/National_Film_Registry "National Film Registry") by the [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress") as being "culturally, historically, or aesthetically significant".[[5]](#cite_note-5) In 2007 the [American Film Institute](/wiki/American_Film_Institute "American Film Institute") ranked *Raging Bull* as the [fourth-greatest American movie of all time](/wiki/AFI%27s_100_Years...100_Movies_(10th_Anniversary_Edition) "AFI's 100 Years...100 Movies (10th Anniversary Edition)").

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raging_Bull&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

In 1941, [Jake LaMotta](/wiki/Jake_LaMotta "Jake LaMotta") is a young up-and-coming [middleweight](/wiki/Middleweight "Middleweight") boxer who suffers his first loss to [Jimmy Reeves](/wiki/Jimmy_Reeves "Jimmy Reeves") after a controversial decision. Jake's brother [Joey](/wiki/Joey_LaMotta "Joey LaMotta") discusses a potential shot for the middleweight title with one of his [Mafia](/wiki/Italian-American_Mafia "Italian-American Mafia") connections, Salvy Batts. However, Jake repeatedly refuses the Mafia's help, wanting to win the championship on his own terms. Jake spots a 15-year-old girl named Vickie at a swimming pool in his [Bronx](/wiki/Bronx "Bronx") neighborhood. He eventually pursues a relationship with her, despite his being already married and her being underage. In 1943, Jake defeats [Sugar Ray Robinson](/wiki/Sugar_Ray_Robinson "Sugar Ray Robinson"), and has a rematch three weeks later. Despite Jake dominating Robinson during the bout, the judges surprisingly rule in favor of Robinson, who Joey feels won only because he was enlisting in the [Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") the following week.

By 1945, Jake marries Vickie, but he is controlling and domineering over her, and suspicious that she has feelings for other men. When she remarks off-hand that his next opponent [Tony Janiro](/wiki/Tony_Janiro "Tony Janiro") is good-looking, Jake flies into a rage, asking her what she meant by that. At the fight with Janiro, with Vickie looking on in horror, Jake makes it a point to brutally disfigure his opponent. At the [Copacabana](/wiki/Copacabana_(nightclub) "Copacabana (nightclub)") afterwards, Joey sees Vickie approach a table with Salvy and his crew. Joey confronts Vickie, who implies that she is dissatisfied in her marriage. Enraged, Joey viciously attacks Salvy in a fight that spills outside the club. Local [mob boss](/wiki/Mob_boss "Mob boss") Tommy Como orders them to patch up, and has Joey tell Jake that if he wants a shot at the championship title, which Como controls, he will have to take a [dive](/wiki/Match_fixing "Match fixing"). Jake thus throws his next match against [Billy Fox](/wiki/Billy_Fox_(boxer) "Billy Fox (boxer)"), but cannot convincingly fake his defeat. He is booed out of the building and shortly thereafter suspended from boxing on suspicion of throwing the fight. By 1949, he is reinstated and wins the middleweight championship title by defeating [Marcel Cerdan](/wiki/Marcel_Cerdan "Marcel Cerdan").

In 1950, Jake becomes increasingly paranoid that Vickie is having an affair. He asks Joey if he has slept with her, which enrages Joey and causes him to leave. After Jake presses Vickie too about this, she sarcastically confesses that she had sex with Joey, Salvy, and Tommy. In a fit of rage, Jake storms into Joey's house and assaults him in front of his wife and children, and knocks Vickie unconscious as well. Vickie returns to their home and threatens to leave, but they reconcile. After defending his championship belt in a grueling 15-round bout against [Laurent Dauthuille](/wiki/Laurent_Dauthuille "Laurent Dauthuille"), he calls his brother to make amends. When Jake does not speak Joey assumes Salvy is on the other end and starts insulting him; Jake hangs up. Estranged from his brother, Jake's career declines, and he loses his title to Sugar Ray Robinson in their [final encounter in 1951](/wiki/Sugar_Ray_Robinson_vs._Jake_LaMotta "Sugar Ray Robinson vs. Jake LaMotta").

By 1956, an aging and overweight Jake has retired and moved with his family to Miami. After he stays out all night at the nightclub he owns, Vickie tells him that she wants a divorce, as well as full custody of their children. She also threatens to call the police if he comes anywhere near them. Soon after, Jake is arrested for admitting underage girls into his nightclub, and he unsuccessfully attempts to bribe his way out of his criminal case by pawning the jewels on his championship belt. In 1957, he is put in [solitary confinement](/wiki/Solitary_confinement "Solitary confinement") awaiting his sentence, where he questions his misfortune and cries in despair. After returning to New York City in 1958 after serving six months, he encounters Joey, whom he forcefully embraces; Joey half-heartedly reciprocates.

In 1964, Jake performs [stand-up comedy](/wiki/Stand-up_comedy "Stand-up comedy") at various clubs. Backstage before a show, LaMotta prepares for his performance by [shadowboxing](/wiki/Shadowboxing "Shadowboxing"), quoting scenes from *[On the Waterfront](/wiki/On_the_Waterfront "On the Waterfront")* and chanting "I'm the boss".

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raging_Bull&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Production")]

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raging_Bull&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Development")]

*Raging Bull* was initiated when [Robert De Niro](/wiki/Robert_De_Niro "Robert De Niro") read the [autobiography](/wiki/Raging_Bull:_My_Story "Raging Bull: My Story") while he was on the set of *[The Godfather Part II](/wiki/The_Godfather_Part_II "The Godfather Part II")*. Although disappointed by the book's writing style, De Niro became fascinated by the character of [Jake LaMotta](/wiki/Jake_LaMotta "Jake LaMotta"). He showed the book to [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese") on the set of *[Alice Doesn't Live Here Anymore](/wiki/Alice_Doesn%27t_Live_Here_Anymore "Alice Doesn't Live Here Anymore")*, with the hope that he would consider the project.[[7]](#cite_note-Biskind_p254-7)

Scorsese repeatedly turned down the opportunity to direct the film, claiming that he had no idea what *Raging Bull* was about, although he had read some of the text. Never a sports fan, when he found out what LaMotta used to do for a living, he said, "A boxer? I don't like boxing...Even as a kid, I always thought that boxing was boring... It was something I couldn't, wouldn't grasp." His overall opinion of sport in general is, "Anything with a ball, no good."[[8]](#cite_note-Biskind_p378-8)

The book was passed on to Mardik Martin, the film's eventual co-screenwriter, who said, "The trouble is the damn thing has been done a hundred times before—a fighter who has trouble with his brother and his wife and the mob is after him." De Niro had even shown the book to producers [Robert Chartoff](/wiki/Robert_Chartoff "Robert Chartoff") and [Irwin Winkler](/wiki/Irwin_Winkler "Irwin Winkler"), who were willing to assist only if Scorsese agreed.[[9]](#cite_note-Biskind_p315-9)

After nearly dying from a [drug overdose](/wiki/Drug_overdose "Drug overdose"), Scorsese agreed to make the film, not only to save his own life but also to save his career. Scorsese began to relate very personally to the story of Jake LaMotta, and in it, he saw how the boxing ring can be "an allegory for whatever you do in life", which for him paralleled moviemaking: "You make movies, you're in the ring each time."[[10]](#cite_note-Thompson_pp.76/77-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Friedman_p115-11)[[12]](#cite_note-villarreal-12)[[13]](#cite_note-torrance-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/2/26/JakeLaMotta_with_DeNiro.jpg/250px-JakeLaMotta_with_DeNiro.jpg)](/wiki/File:JakeLaMotta_with_DeNiro.jpg)

[Robert De Niro](/wiki/Robert_De_Niro "Robert De Niro") in training with the real [Jake LaMotta](/wiki/Jake_LaMotta "Jake LaMotta")

Preparation for the film began when Scorsese shot some 8 mm color footage featuring De Niro boxing in a ring. One night, when the footage was being shown to De Niro, [Michael Chapman](/wiki/Michael_Chapman_(cinematographer) "Michael Chapman (cinematographer)") and his friend and mentor, the British director [Michael Powell](/wiki/Michael_Powell "Michael Powell"), Powell pointed out that the color of the gloves at the time would have been only maroon, oxblood or black. It is one of the reasons that Scorsese chose to film *Raging Bull* in [black and white](/wiki/Black_and_white "Black and white"). Other reasons were to distinguish the film from color films at the time, and to acknowledge the problem of fading color film stock—an issue that Scorsese recognized.[[14]](#cite_note-Biskind_p389-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Total_Film_pp180-181-15)[[16]](#cite_note-Thompson_p80-16) Scorsese attended two matches at [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden "Madison Square Garden") to aid his research, picking up on minor but essential details, such as the blood sponge and subsequently, the blood on the ropes (which would be used in the film).[[16]](#cite_note-Thompson_p80-16)

Multiple titles were considered for *Raging Bull*, including *Prizefighter* and *The Jake La Motta Story*. Scorsese stated that *Prizefighter* was his favorite title, but did not select it, for he was afraid that people would think that the film was solely about boxing.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson201183-17)

### Screenplay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raging_Bull&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Screenplay")]

Under the guidance of Chartoff and Winkler, [Mardik Martin](/wiki/Mardik_Martin "Mardik Martin") was asked to start writing the screenplay.[[18]](#cite_note-Biskind_p379-18) According to De Niro, under no circumstances would [United Artists](/wiki/United_Artists "United Artists") accept Martin's script.[[19]](#cite_note-Biskind_pp384-385-19) The story was based on the vision of journalist [Pete Hamill](/wiki/Pete_Hamill "Pete Hamill") of a 1930s and 1940s style, when boxing was known as "the great dark prince of sports". De Niro, however, was unimpressed when he finished reading the first draft.[[20]](#cite_note-Baxter_pp186-189-20)

*[Taxi Driver](/wiki/Taxi_Driver "Taxi Driver")* screenwriter [Paul Schrader](/wiki/Paul_Schrader "Paul Schrader") was swiftly brought in to rewrite the script around August 1978.[[20]](#cite_note-Baxter_pp186-189-20) Some of the changes that Schrader made to the script include a rewrite of the scene with the undercooked steak, and the inclusion of LaMotta seen masturbating in a [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida") cell.

The character of LaMotta's brother Joey was finally added, previously absent from Martin's script.[[19]](#cite_note-Biskind_pp384-385-19)[[20]](#cite_note-Baxter_pp186-189-20) United Artists saw a massive improvement on the quality of the script. However, its chief executives [Steven Bach](/wiki/Steven_Bach "Steven Bach") and David Field met with Scorsese, De Niro and producer [Irwin Winkler](/wiki/Irwin_Winkler "Irwin Winkler") in November 1978 to say that they were worried that the content would be [X-rated](/wiki/X-rated "X-rated") material and have no chance of finding an audience.[[14]](#cite_note-Biskind_p389-14)

According to Scorsese, the script was left to him and De Niro, and they spent two-and-a-half weeks on the island of [Saint Martin](/wiki/Saint_Martin_(island) "Saint Martin (island)") extensively re-building the content of the film.[[10]](#cite_note-Thompson_pp.76/77-10) The most significant change would be the scene in which LaMotta fixes his television and accuses his wife of having an affair.

Other changes included the removal of Jake and Joey's father, the reduction of [organized crime](/wiki/Organized_crime "Organized crime")'s role in the story, and a major rewrite of LaMotta's fight with Tony Janiro.[[21]](#cite_note-Biskind_p390-21)[[22]](#cite_note-Baxter_p193-22) They were also responsible for the end sequence in which LaMotta is alone in his dressing room, quoting "I could have been a contender" from *[On the Waterfront](/wiki/On_the_Waterfront "On the Waterfront")*.[[22]](#cite_note-Baxter_p193-22) An extract of *[Richard III](/wiki/Richard_III_(play) "Richard III (play)")* had been considered, but [Michael Powell](/wiki/Michael_Powell "Michael Powell") thought that it would be a bad decision within the context of an American film.[[10]](#cite_note-Thompson_pp.76/77-10) According to [Steven Bach](/wiki/Steven_Bach "Steven Bach"), the first two screenwriters (Martin and Schrader) would receive credit, but since there was no payment to the writer's guild on the script, De Niro and Scorsese's work remained uncredited.[[22]](#cite_note-Baxter_p193-22)

### Casting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raging_Bull&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Casting")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Cathy_Moriarty_and_Joe_Pesci%2C_1980.jpg/250px-Cathy_Moriarty_and_Joe_Pesci%2C_1980.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cathy_Moriarty_and_Joe_Pesci,_1980.jpg)

Cathy Moriarty (left) and Joe Pesci (right) on the set

One of Scorsese's trademarks was casting many actors and actresses new to the profession.[[23]](#cite_note-Evans_p65-23) De Niro, who was already committed to play Jake LaMotta, began to help Scorsese track down unfamiliar names to play his on-screen brother Joey and wife Vikki.[[24]](#cite_note-Evans_p61-24)[[25]](#cite_note-Baxter_pp196-201-25) The role of Joey LaMotta was the first to be cast. De Niro was watching a low-budget television film called *[The Death Collector](/wiki/The_Death_Collector "The Death Collector")* when he saw the part of a young career criminal played by a relatively-unknown [Joe Pesci](/wiki/Joe_Pesci "Joe Pesci") as an ideal candidate. Prior to receiving a call from De Niro and Scorsese for the proposal to star in the film, Pesci had not worked in film for four years and was working at an Italian restaurant in [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey").

The role of Vikki (spelled "Vickie" in the final film), Jake's second wife, had interest across the board, but it was Pesci who suggested the unknown [Cathy Moriarty](/wiki/Cathy_Moriarty "Cathy Moriarty") from a picture that he saw at a New Jersey disco.[[25]](#cite_note-Baxter_pp196-201-25) Both De Niro and Scorsese believed that Moriarty, at 18 years old, could portray the role after meeting with her on several occasions and noticing her husky voice "and a confident sexuality beyond her years." The duo had to prove to the [Screen Actors Guild](/wiki/Screen_Actors_Guild "Screen Actors Guild") that she was right for the role when [Cis Corman](/wiki/Cis_Corman "Cis Corman") showed 10 comparing pictures of Moriarty and the real Vikki LaMotta for proof that she had a resemblance.[[25]](#cite_note-Baxter_pp196-201-25)

Moriarty was asked to take a screen test, which she managed—partly aided by some improvised lines from De Niro—after some confusion wondering why the crew was filming her take. Joe Pesci also persuaded his former show-biz pal and co-star in *[The Death Collector](/wiki/The_Death_Collector "The Death Collector")*, [Frank Vincent](/wiki/Frank_Vincent "Frank Vincent"), to audition for the role of Salvy Batts. Following a successful audition and screen test, Vincent received the call to say that he had received the part.[[26]](#cite_note-Evans_pp.65/66-26) [Charles Scorsese](/wiki/Charles_Scorsese "Charles Scorsese"), the director's father, made his film debut as Tommy Como's cousin Charlie.[[26]](#cite_note-Evans_pp.65/66-26)

While in the midst of practicing a [Bronx accent](/wiki/Bronx_accent "Bronx accent") and preparing for his role, De Niro met with both LaMotta and his ex-wife Vikki on separate occasions. Vikki, who lived in Florida, told stories about her life with her former husband, and showed old home movies (that later inspired a similar sequence to be done for the film).[[15]](#cite_note-Total_Film_pp180-181-15)[[27]](#cite_note-Baxter_p192-27)

Jake LaMotta, on the other hand, served as his trainer, accompanied by [Al Silvani](/wiki/Al_Silvani "Al Silvani") as coach at the Gramercy club in New York City, getting him into shape. The actor found that boxing came naturally to him; he entered as a middleweight boxer, winning two of his three fights in a [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") ring dubbed "young LaMotta" by the commentator. According to Jake LaMotta, De Niro was one of the top 20 best middleweight boxers of all time.[[15]](#cite_note-Total_Film_pp180-181-15)[[25]](#cite_note-Baxter_pp196-201-25)

### Principal photography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raging_Bull&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Principal photography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/f/fd/Filming_the_boxing_scenes.jpg/250px-Filming_the_boxing_scenes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Filming_the_boxing_scenes.jpg)

Filming of the boxing scenes with director, Scorsese (center left, with beard) and the director of photography, Michael Chapman (center right, with white shirt)

According to the sound mixer, Michael Evje, the film began shooting at the [Los Angeles Olympic Auditorium](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Olympic_Auditorium "Los Angeles Olympic Auditorium") on April 16, 1979. [Grips](/wiki/Grip_(job) "Grip (job)") hung huge curtains of black [duvetyne](/wiki/Duvetyne "Duvetyne") on all four sides of the ring area to contain the artificial smoke used extensively for visual effect.

On May 7, the production moved to the [Culver City](/wiki/Culver_City "Culver City") studio, Stage 3, and filmed there until the middle of June. Scorsese made it clear during filming that he did not appreciate the traditional way of showing fights from the spectators' view.[[16]](#cite_note-Thompson_p80-16) He insisted that one camera operated by the Director of Photography, [Michael Chapman](/wiki/Michael_Chapman_(cinematographer) "Michael Chapman (cinematographer)"), would be placed inside of the ring, as he would play the role of an opponent keeping out of the way of other fighters, so that viewers could see the emotions of the fighters, including those of Jake.[[25]](#cite_note-Baxter_pp196-201-25)

The precise moves of the boxers were to be done as dance routines from the information of a book about dance instructors in the mode of [Arthur Murray](/wiki/Arthur_Murray "Arthur Murray"). A punching bag in the middle of the ring was used by De Niro between takes before he aggressively came straight on to do the next scene.[[25]](#cite_note-Baxter_pp196-201-25)[[28]](#cite_note-Thompson_and_Christie_pp83-84-28) The initial five-week schedule for the shooting of the boxing scenes took longer than expected, putting Scorsese under pressure.[[25]](#cite_note-Baxter_pp196-201-25)

According to Scorsese, production of the film was closed for nearly four months with the entire crew being paid, so that De Niro could go on a binge-eating trip around northern Italy and France.[[15]](#cite_note-Total_Film_pp180-181-15)[[28]](#cite_note-Thompson_and_Christie_pp83-84-28) When he arrived in the United States, his weight had increased from 145 to 215 pounds (66 to 97 kg).[[25]](#cite_note-Baxter_pp196-201-25) The scenes with the heftier Jake LaMotta—which include announcing his retirement from boxing and LaMotta in a Florida cell—were completed seven-to-eight weeks later when approaching Christmas 1979, so as not to aggravate the health issues that were affecting De Niro's posture, breathing and talking.[[25]](#cite_note-Baxter_pp196-201-25)[[28]](#cite_note-Thompson_and_Christie_pp83-84-28)[[29]](#cite_note-Baxter_p83-29)

According to Evje, Jake's nightclub sequence was filmed in a closed [San Pedro](/wiki/San_Pedro,_California "San Pedro, California") club on December 3. The jail cell head-banging scene was shot on a constructed set, with De Niro asking for minimal crew to be present. There was not even a boom operator present.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The final sequence, in which Jake LaMotta is in front of his mirror, was filmed on the last day of shooting, requiring 19 takes, with only the 13th being used for the film. Scorsese wanted to have an atmosphere that would be so cold that the words would have an impact as he tries to come to terms with his relationship with his brother.[[10]](#cite_note-Thompson_pp.76/77-10)

### Post-production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raging_Bull&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Post-production")]

The editing of *Raging Bull* began when production was temporarily put on hold and was completed in 1980.[[28]](#cite_note-Thompson_and_Christie_pp83-84-28)[[30]](#cite_note-Biskind_p399-30) Scorsese worked with the editor [Thelma Schoonmaker](/wiki/Thelma_Schoonmaker "Thelma Schoonmaker") to achieve a final cut of the film. Their main decision was to abandon Schrader's idea of LaMotta's nightclub act interweaving with the flashback of his youth, and instead followed the lines of a single flashback, in which only scenes of LaMotta practicing his stand-up would remain [bookending](/wiki/Bookend_scene "Bookend scene") the film.[[31]](#cite_note-Baxter_p90-31)

A sound mix arranged by [Frank Warner](/wiki/Frank_Warner_(sound_editor) "Frank Warner (sound editor)") was a delicate process that took six months.[[30]](#cite_note-Biskind_p399-30) According to Scorsese, the sound on *Raging Bull* was difficult because each punch, camera shot and flash bulb would be different. Also, there was the issue of trying to balance the quality between scenes featuring dialogue and those involving boxing (which were done in [Dolby Stereo](/wiki/Dolby_Stereo "Dolby Stereo")).[[28]](#cite_note-Thompson_and_Christie_pp83-84-28) *Raging Bull* went through a test screening in front of a small audience including the chief executives of United Artists, [Steven Bach](/wiki/Steven_Bach "Steven Bach") and [Andy Albeck](/wiki/Andy_Albeck "Andy Albeck"). The screening was shown at the [M-G-M](/wiki/M-G-M "M-G-M") screening room in New York in July 1980. Albeck praised Scorsese by calling him a "true artist".[[30]](#cite_note-Biskind_p399-30)

According to the producers Robert Chartoff and Irwin Winkler, matters were made worse when United Artists decided not to distribute the film but no other studios were interested when they attempted to sell the rights.[[30]](#cite_note-Biskind_p399-30) Scorsese made no secret that *Raging Bull* would be his "[Hollywood](/wiki/Cinema_in_the_United_States "Cinema in the United States") swan song" and he took unusual care of its rights during post-production.[[11]](#cite_note-Friedman_p115-11) Scorsese threatened to remove his credit from the film if he was not allowed to sort a reel that obscured the name of a whisky brand ([Cutty Sark](/wiki/Cutty_Sark_(whisky) "Cutty Sark (whisky)")) that was heard in a scene. The work was completed four days shy of the premiere.[[32]](#cite_note-Baxter_p204-32)

In 2012, *Raging Bull* was voted by the [Motion Picture Editors Guild](/wiki/Motion_Picture_Editors_Guild "Motion Picture Editors Guild") as the best-edited film in history.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Copyright litigation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raging_Bull&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Copyright litigation")]

Paula Petrella, heir to Frank Petrello, whose works were allegedly sources for the film, filed for copyright infringement in 2009 based on MGM's 1991 copyright renewal of the film. In 2014, the Supreme Court held, in *[Petrella v. Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer, Inc.](/wiki/Petrella_v._Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer,_Inc. "Petrella v. Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer, Inc.")*, that Petrella's suit survived MGM's defense of "[laches](/wiki/Laches_(equity) "Laches (equity)")", the legal doctrine that protects defendants from unreasonable delays by potential plaintiffs. The case was remanded to lower courts, meaning that Petrella could receive a decision on the merits of her claim.[[34]](#cite_note-34) MGM settled with Petrella in 2015.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raging_Bull&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Box office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raging_Bull&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Box office")]

The brew of violence and anger, combined with the lack of a proper advertising campaign, led to the film's lukewarm box-office intake of $23 million, compared to its $18 million budget. By the time it left theaters, it earned $10.1 million in [theatrical rentals](/wiki/Gross_rental "Gross rental") (equivalent to $32.2 million in 2024).[[36]](#cite_note-36) Scorsese became concerned for his future, and worried that producers and studios may refuse to finance his films.[[37]](#cite_note-Evans_pp124-129-37) According to [Box Office Mojo](/wiki/Box_Office_Mojo "Box Office Mojo"), the film grossed $23,383,987 in domestic theaters (equivalent to $74.5 million in 2024).[[38]](#cite_note-BOMJ-38)

### Critical response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raging_Bull&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Critical response")]

When it premiered in New York City on November 14, 1980, the release of *Raging Bull* was met with polarized reviews, but the film would receive widespread critical acclaim, and is widely regarded as one of Scorsese's best works.[[30]](#cite_note-Biskind_p399-30)[[31]](#cite_note-Baxter_p90-31)

On the [review aggregator](/wiki/Review_aggregator "Review aggregator") [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), the film has an approval rating of 92% based on 151 reviews, with an average rating of 9.00/10. The site's critical consensus reads, "Arguably Martin Scorsese's and Robert De Niro's finest film, *Raging Bull* is often painful to watch, but it's a searing, powerful work about an unsympathetic hero."[[39]](#cite_note-39) [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), which assigns a weighted average, gave it a score of 90 out of 100, based on 28 reviews, indicating "universal acclaim".[[40]](#cite_note-40) Audiences polled by [CinemaScore](/wiki/CinemaScore "CinemaScore") gave the film an average grade of "A" on a scale of A+ to F.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

[Jack Kroll](/wiki/Jack_Kroll "Jack Kroll") of *[Newsweek](/wiki/Newsweek "Newsweek")* called *Raging Bull* the "best movie of the year".[[30]](#cite_note-Biskind_p399-30)

Critic for *[The New Yorker](/wiki/The_New_Yorker "The New Yorker")*, [Pauline Kael](/wiki/Pauline_Kael "Pauline Kael") described the film not as a biographical film about a boxer, but as a "biography of the *genre* of prize fighting" further saying the film is "tabloid grand opera" and "overripe, ready for canonization" in which Scorsese puts his "unmediated obsessions on the screen."[[42]](#cite_note-42)

[Vincent Canby](/wiki/Vincent_Canby "Vincent Canby") of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* said that Scorsese "has made his most ambitious film as well as his finest", and went on to praise Moriarty's debut performance, saying, "Either she is one of the film finds of the decade or Mr. Scorsese is [Svengali](/wiki/Svengali "Svengali"). Perhaps both."[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[37]](#cite_note-Evans_pp124-129-37)

*[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* praised De Niro's performance because "much of *Raging Bull* exists because of the possibilities it offers De Niro to display his own explosive art".[[37]](#cite_note-Evans_pp124-129-37)

[Steven Jenkins](/wiki/Steve_Jenkins_(author) "Steve Jenkins (author)") from the [British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute")'s (BFI) magazine *Monthly Film Journal* said that "*Raging Bull* may prove to be Scorsese's finest achievement to date".[[37]](#cite_note-Evans_pp124-129-37)

The Oscars were held the day after President [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") was shot by [John Hinckley](/wiki/John_Hinckley "John Hinckley"), who did it as an attempt to impress [Jodie Foster](/wiki/Jodie_Foster "Jodie Foster"), who played a child prostitute in another of Scorsese's famous films, *[Taxi Driver](/wiki/Taxi_Driver "Taxi Driver")* (which also starred De Niro).[[46]](#cite_note-46) Out of fear of being attacked, Scorsese went to the ceremony with [FBI](/wiki/FBI "FBI") bodyguards disguised as guests who escorted him before the announcement of the [Academy Award for Best Picture](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Picture "Academy Award for Best Picture") was made (the winner being Robert Redford's *[Ordinary People](/wiki/Ordinary_People "Ordinary People")*).

The [Los Angeles Film Critics Association](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Film_Critics_Association "Los Angeles Film Critics Association") voted *Raging Bull* the best film of 1980, and De Niro best actor. The [National Board of Review](/wiki/National_Board_of_Review "National Board of Review") also voted De Niro best actor and Pesci best supporting actor. The [Berlin International Film Festival](/wiki/Berlin_International_Film_Festival "Berlin International Film Festival") chose *Raging Bull* to open the festival in 1981.[[37]](#cite_note-Evans_pp124-129-37)

The 2012 [Parajanov-Vartanov Institute](/wiki/Parajanov-Vartanov_Institute "Parajanov-Vartanov Institute") Award honored screenwriter [Mardik Martin](/wiki/Mardik_Martin "Mardik Martin") "for the mastery of his pen on iconic American films" *Mean Streets* and *Raging Bull*.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

### Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raging_Bull&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Legacy")]

By the end of the 1980s, *Raging Bull* had cemented its reputation as a [modern classic](/wiki/Cult_film "Cult film"). It was voted the best film of the 1980s in numerous critics' polls, and is regularly pointed to as both Scorsese's best film and one of the finest American films ever made.[[48]](#cite_note-Walker_p.561-48) Several prominent critics, among them [Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert"), declared the film to be an instant classic and the consummation of Scorsese's earlier promise. Ebert proclaimed it the best film of the 1980s,[[49]](#cite_note-Chicago_Sun-Times-49) and one of the ten greatest films of all time.[[50]](#cite_note-50) The film has been deemed "culturally, historically, and aesthetically significant" by the United States [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress") and was selected for preservation in the [National Film Registry](/wiki/National_Film_Registry "National Film Registry") in 1990.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

*Raging Bull* was listed by *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine as one of the *[All-TIME 100 Movies](/wiki/Time%27s_All-TIME_100_Movies "Time's All-TIME 100 Movies")*.[[52]](#cite_note-52) *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* magazine ranked the film number 39 on their list of the 50 greatest movies.[[53]](#cite_note-53) *Raging Bull* is fifth on *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")'s* list of the 100 Greatest Movies of All Time.[[54]](#cite_note-54) The film tied with *[Bicycle Thieves](/wiki/Bicycle_Thieves "Bicycle Thieves")* and *[Vertigo](/wiki/Vertigo_(film) "Vertigo (film)")* at number 6 on *[Sight & Sound](/wiki/Sight_%26_Sound "Sight & Sound")*'s 2002 poll of the greatest movies.[[55]](#cite_note-55) When *Sight & Sound*'s directors' and critics' lists from that year are combined, *Raging Bull* gets the most votes of any movie that has been produced since 1975.[[56]](#cite_note-56) In 2002, [Film4](/wiki/Film4 "Film4") held a poll of the 100 Greatest Movies, on which *Raging Bull* was voted in at number 20.[[57]](#cite_note-57) *[Halliwell's Film Guide](/wiki/Halliwell%27s_Film_Guide "Halliwell's Film Guide")*, a British film guide, placed *Raging Bull* seventh in a poll naming their selection for the "Top 1,000 Movies".[[58]](#cite_note-58) *[TV Guide](/wiki/TV_Guide "TV Guide")* also included the film on their list of the 50 best movies.[[59]](#cite_note-59) *[Movieline](/wiki/Movieline "Movieline")* magazine included the film on its list of the 100 best movies.[[60]](#cite_note-60) [Leonard Maltin](/wiki/Leonard_Maltin "Leonard Maltin") included *Raging Bull* on his *100 Must-See Films of the 20th Century* list.[[61]](#cite_note-61) *Video Detective* also included the film on its list of the top 100 movies of all time.[[62]](#cite_note-62) [Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") named "Robert De Niro's transformation from sleek boxer to paunchy nightclub owner in *Raging Bull*" as one of the *100 Greatest Movie Moments*.[[63]](#cite_note-63) The [National Society of Film Critics](/wiki/National_Society_of_Film_Critics "National Society of Film Critics") ranked it #75 on their *100 Essential Films* list.[[64]](#cite_note-64) *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* magazine ranked it #6 on their list of the *100 Maverick Movies in the Last 100 Years*.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

A 1997 readers poll conducted by the *[L.A. Daily News](/wiki/L.A._Daily_News "L.A. Daily News")* ranked the film #64 on a list of the greatest American movies.[[66]](#cite_note-66) In 2006, the [Writers Guild of America](/wiki/Writers_Guild_of_America "Writers Guild of America") named the film as the 76th best screenplay of all time.[[67]](#cite_note-67) *Raging Bull* is #7 on *[Time Out Film Guide's](/wiki/Time_Out_(magazine) "Time Out (magazine)")* "Centenary Top 100" list,[[68]](#cite_note-68) and it also tied at #16 (with *[Lawrence of Arabia](/wiki/Lawrence_of_Arabia_(film) "Lawrence of Arabia (film)")*) on their 1998 readers poll.[[69]](#cite_note-69) In 2008, *[Empire](/wiki/Empire_(film_magazine) "Empire (film magazine)")* magazine held a poll of *The 500 Greatest Movies of All Time*, taking votes from 10,000 readers, 150 film makers, and 50 film critics in which *Raging Bull* was placed at number 11.[[70]](#cite_note-70) It was also placed on a similar list of 1000 movies by *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*.[[71]](#cite_note-71) In 2010, *[Total Film](/wiki/Total_Film "Total Film")* selected the film as one of *The 100 Greatest Movies of All Time*.[[72]](#cite_note-72) [FilmSite.org](/wiki/Filmsite.org "Filmsite.org"), a [subsidiary](/wiki/Subsidiary "Subsidiary") of [American Movie Classics](/wiki/American_Movie_Classics "American Movie Classics"), placed *Raging Bull* on their list of the 100 greatest movies.[[73]](#cite_note-73) Additionally, Films101.com ranked the film as the 17th best movie of all time in a list of the 10,790 most notable.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

In 2012, the [Motion Picture Editors Guild](/wiki/Motion_Picture_Editors_Guild "Motion Picture Editors Guild") listed the film as the best-edited film of all time based on a survey of its membership.[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76) In the [2012 *Sight & Sound* polls](/wiki/The_Sight_%26_Sound_Greatest_Films_of_All_Time_2012 "The Sight & Sound Greatest Films of All Time 2012"), it was ranked the 53rd-greatest film ever made in the critics' poll[[77]](#cite_note-bfi-77) and 12th in the directors' poll.[[78]](#cite_note-78) Contemporaries of Scorsese, like [Francis Ford Coppola](/wiki/Francis_Ford_Coppola "Francis Ford Coppola"), have included it routinely in their lists for favorite films of all time. In 2015, *Raging Bull* ranked 29th on [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC")'s "100 Greatest American Films" list, voted on by film critics from around the world.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

## Soundtrack

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raging_Bull&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Soundtrack")]

Martin Scorsese decided to assemble a soundtrack made of music that was popular at the time using his personal collection of [78s](/wiki/78s "78s"). With the help of [Robbie Robertson](/wiki/Robbie_Robertson "Robbie Robertson"), the songs were carefully chosen so they would be the ones that a person would hear on the radio, at the pool or in bars and clubs which reflected the mood of that particular era.[[84]](#cite_note-Thompson_p83-84)[[85]](#cite_note-Evans_p88-85) Some lyrics from songs would also be slipped into some dialogue. The [Intermezzo](/wiki/Intermezzo "Intermezzo") from *[Cavalleria rusticana](/wiki/Cavalleria_rusticana "Cavalleria rusticana")* by Italian composer [Pietro Mascagni](/wiki/Pietro_Mascagni "Pietro Mascagni") would serve as the main theme to *Raging Bull* after a successful try-out by Scorsese and the editor, [Thelma Schoonmaker](/wiki/Thelma_Schoonmaker "Thelma Schoonmaker"), over the film's opening titles.[[85]](#cite_note-Evans_p88-85) Two other Mascagni pieces were used in the film: the [Barcarolle](/wiki/Barcarolle "Barcarolle") from *[Silvano](/wiki/Silvano_(opera) "Silvano (opera)")*, and the Intermezzo (*Ratcliff's Dream*) from *[Guglielmo Ratcliff](/wiki/Guglielmo_Ratcliff "Guglielmo Ratcliff")*.[[86]](#cite_note-86) A two-CD soundtrack was released in 2005, long after the film was released, because of earlier difficulties obtaining rights for many of the songs, which Scorsese selected from his childhood memories growing up in New York.

## Dispute over sequel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Raging_Bull&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Dispute over sequel")]

Main article: [*The Bronx Bull*](/wiki/The_Bronx_Bull "The Bronx Bull")

In 2006, *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* reported that [Sunset Pictures](/wiki/Sunset_Pictures "Sunset Pictures") was developing a combination sequel and prequel film entitled *Raging Bull II: Continuing the Story of Jake LaMotta*, chronicling LaMotta's life before and after the events of the original film, as told in the memoir of the same name.[[87]](#cite_note-87) Filming began on June 15, 2012, with [William Forsythe](/wiki/William_Forsythe_(actor) "William Forsythe (actor)") as the older LaMotta and [Mojean Aria](/wiki/Mojean_Aria "Mojean Aria") as the younger version (before the events of the first film).[[88]](#cite_note-88) The film, directed by Martin Guigui, also stars [Joe Mantegna](/wiki/Joe_Mantegna "Joe Mantegna"), [Tom Sizemore](/wiki/Tom_Sizemore "Tom Sizemore"), [Penelope Ann Miller](/wiki/Penelope_Ann_Miller "Penelope Ann Miller"), [Natasha Henstridge](/wiki/Natasha_Henstridge "Natasha Henstridge"), [Alicia Witt](/wiki/Alicia_Witt "Alicia Witt"), [Ray Wise](/wiki/Ray_Wise "Ray Wise"), [Harry Hamlin](/wiki/Harry_Hamlin "Harry Hamlin"), and [James Russo](/wiki/James_Russo "James Russo") as [Rocky Graziano](/wiki/Rocky_Graziano "Rocky Graziano").[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[90]](#cite_note-90) In July 2012, [MGM](/wiki/MGM "MGM"), owners of United Artists, filed a lawsuit against LaMotta and the producers of the new film to block it from being released. MGM argued that they had the rights to make any authorized sequel to the original book, tracing their claim back to an agreement LaMotta and co-author Peter Savage made with Chartoff-Winkler, producers of the original film. MGM argued that the defendants were publicly claiming the film to be a sequel to the original film, which they said could "tarnish" the original film's reputation.[[91]](#cite_note-91) In August 2012, the suit was settled, with producers of the new film retitling it *[The Bronx Bull](/wiki/The_Bronx_Bull "The Bronx Bull")* and agreeing not to market it as a sequel to *Raging Bull*.[[92]](#cite_note-92) The film was released in 2016.
